dialogue.utterance: Hi sir, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What's the main issue you're experiencing today?
I can't catch my breath. It's been getting worse when I'm laying down.
So, have there been any other changes you've noticed with the shortness of breath?
I've had swelling in my legs and a cough with white, foamy stuff.
Sir, can you speak with me normally?
Your airway is clear and open. Now, let's check your breathing pattern.
You're breathing fast-about 36 breaths per minute-and your effort seems high.
You're sitting upright to breathe better. Is that correct?
Yeah, I can't lie flat. It makes it feel even worse.
That's called orthopnea. Thanks for explaining. Can you squeeze both of my hands tightly?
(Squeezes hands firmly)
Strong bilateral grip. No signs of weakness in either hand. Let's check your skin now.
Your skin looks pale and is a bit clammy. Any change in skin color or temperature?
No, I don't notice temperature changes.
Can you tell me what day it is today?
It's Sunday.
Good. You're alert and oriented. How about your last meal?
I had breakfast about an hour ago.
Your heart rate is 110 beats per minute now and still in a regular pattern.
Your respiratory rate has dropped a little to 36 now.
We just took your blood pressure and it's 156/117.
Pulse oximeter shows saturation at 93%, a bit low.
Your EKG shows sinus tachycardia and premature ventricular contractions.
Blood glucose is zero, so we'll watch for possible changes in how you feel.
You mentioned having chronic heart failure. Can you tell me a bit more?
I was running low on my prescribed heart pills and haven't been taking them regularly for a few days.
Do you have any other symptoms, like fever or chest pain, nausea or vomiting?
No fever, pain, nausea. Just the swelling and shortness of breath.
And do you have a previous history of high blood pressure, heart problems, or diabetes?
Yes. I have high blood pressure, heart failure, and type 2 diabetes.
What medications are you currently taking?
My pills are furosemide, metoprolol, atorvastatin, and aspirin.
Are you allergic to any medications or foods?
No known allergies.
Any signs of bruising, rashes, or new skin changes on your body?
Nothing unusual.
Palpation shows normal skin turgor and firm grip. However, your lower legs have 2+ pitting edema.
Your skin is still cool to the touch. Any recent injuries or trauma?
No trauma. Just the swelling.
Your neck veins appear normal, no swelling, and your pupils are equal and reactive.
Your mouth shows no signs of blockage or swelling. Any teeth injuries or sore throat?
No sore throat. Everything feels normal.
Your chest looks fine, and breath sounds are clear at the tops but decreased at the base.
No wheezing noted. Let's take a closer look with some monitoring.
I'm placing a four-lead ECG monitor for continuous heart rhythm surveillance. It will feel like a watch being put on your wrist and chest.
Okay.
Now, I'll record a 12-lead EKG for a detailed assessment of your heart function in a moment.
We'll start an intravenous line to give you medication if needed. That should be in your arm.
The IV was successfully started in your left antecubital vein. It's secure and ready.
I will now give you some nitroglycerin under your tongue to help with the shortness of breath.
Next, we will give you a dose of furosemide intravenously to help with the fluid buildup.
I will give a second dose of nitroglycerin now.
I am calling ahead to notify the hospital. We have a CHF patient in semi-Fowler's position being transported to [COMPANY_NAME].
We placed the patient securely onto the stretcher using shoulder and lap belts to maintain semi-Fowler's position during transport.
The patient is now secured for transport and is breathing through a non-rebreather mask at 15 liters per minute.
The medications given were his double daily dose and didn't significantly improve his breathing distress or blood pressure.
Vital signs at the first check were pulse 114, breathing 39, BP 162/99, and SpO2 at 98%.
Second check: pulse of 111, breathing 29 per minute, BP 174/110, and SpO2 remained stable at 97%.
Final check showed stable vital signs: BP 188/107, pulse 107, breathing 28, and SpO2 98%.
ETCO2 monitoring was done, and it showed a tachypneic waveform with normocapnia.
We rechecked medications and interventions. No changes were made.
We reassessed for pain again, and the patient reported no pain throughout.
Reassessed the patient's breathing and tripod position, and we will transport in the same secured position.
All equipment double-checked for secure placement on the stretcher and during transit.
Your abdomen appears distended but without tenderness or masses.
You don't have any open wounds or dislocations in your arms or legs, just the swelling.
Strength and coordination were normal in both arms, and you were able to grip both of my fingers.
Your back showed no abnormalities, and you moved comfortably when asked.
The patient is alert and ready for hospital transfer.
He is stable enough for transport and monitoring systems are in place.
Depending on his condition on arrival, he may require supplemental care steps while at the ER.
A pre-arrival report was shared with the receiving facility.
No recent urination reported and stools were described as normal.
Redid full medical history and kept a record of new symptoms.
We took a final round of vitals before hospital handoff.
Pulse stable, no efficiency changes noted in breathing pattern since earlier assessments.
No changes in glucose levels from previous reading-still at zero.
Verified all administered drugs: furosemide and sublingual nitroglycerin.
In coordination with hospital staff, we will transport the patient directly to the emergency department at [COMPANY_NAME].
All safety protocols used to ensure transport in proper airway and vascular management is followed.
Transport initiated with continuous non-rebreather and heart rhythm monitoring.
Transport is prepared and underway with vital signs reported for the attending team.
Re-examing neck vein patterns and found JVD is absent during reassessment.
Reconfirmed edema in lower extremities, skin remains cool and pale.
Nothing changed.
We will reassess any changes in BP during transport, as readings have seen higher systolic pressure.
No changes in motor function since initial evaluation, and grip strength remains unchanged.
No signs of new onset conditions like sudden headache or other neurological changes.
Patient has remained fully cooperative and alert during the entire assessment and transport.
No changes in pulse stability and rhythm has not showed new significant issues.
Now initiating hospital handover and transferring all data and equipment to the receiving team.
We are at the hospital and have placed the patient onto the hospital stretcher.
All equipment and documentation have been handed over to the nursing team for further care.
Vital signs were reported before transfer and the patient's current condition is fully documented with notes for continuity.
Overall, patient condition is stable and hospital team is aware of baseline vitals and all interventions administered.
No new symptoms reported, and patient handover has been smoothly executed.
CHF management is planned with fluid monitoring, and he will be reevaluated for possible admission.
This concludes patient care in the field. All systems are powered down and rewrapped now.
All transport work concluded without incident.
Ready for documentation and debrief.
